dialect.e: From Middle French dialecte, from Latin dialectos, dialectus, from Ancient Greek διάλεκτος (diálektos, “conversation, the language of a country or a place or a nation, the local idiom which derives from a dominant language”), from διαλέγομαι (dialégomai, “to participate in a dialogue”), from διά ...

s.def: a deal, a bargain (a favourable transaction)
the; definite article, masculine and feminine singular, plural
examples: De man
De vrouw
Ze namen drie biertjes de man.
We betaalden vijftien euro de neus.

de.e: An unstressed variety of Middle Dutch die. See die for more information.

s.def: neuter singular of die (“that”); referring to a thing or a person further away.
examples: dat huis
dat kind
Wat is dat?

–Voordat je weggaat, zet het afval buiten alsjeblieft!
–Oké, ik zal dat (doen).
Ik ken een meisje dat dat kan.

s.def: that, those (regardless of gender and number)
examples: Dat is een man.
Dat zijn mannen.
Ik zag dat het goed was.
Ik begrijp waarom dat ze dat zeggen:[…]

dat.e:
From Middle Dutch dat, from Old Dutch that, from Proto-Germanic *þat, neuter form of *sa.
Cognate with German das, English that.

s.def: that; referring to a thing or a person further away.
examples: die boom
die vrouw

s.def: those; referring to things or people further away.
examples: die vensters

s.def: a certain, a particular; some; this; referring to a thing or a person invisible or unknown to the audience.
examples: Die vrouw vraagt als iemand aardvruchten wil kopen.
Ik heb die wagen geslagen.
Ik weet niet welke ik moet kiezen. Deze is mooier, maar die is goedkoper.
Die zien er veel mooier uit dan de mijne.
Ik ken geen mensen die dat kunnen.
Oh, maar ik ken iemand die dat wel kan!

die.e: From Middle Dutch die, a merger of Old Dutch thie, thē, thia, thiu and similar forms of the demonstrative. As in Old High German ther, der it replaced the original masculine and feminine nominative forms from Proto-Germanic *sa.

s.def: a décade, 'week' of ten days in the French republican calendar; hence any ten consecutive days
a set of ten book volumes, as part of a larger opus
a decade, period of ten years

decade.e: Borrowed from French décade (“period of ten days”), cognate with German Dekade etc. In the sense “period of ten days” influenced by English decade; this meaning is seldom found outside poor translations from English.

examples: De doctrine stelt duidelijk dat...

doctrine.e: From Middle Dutch doctrine, from Middle French doctrine, from Latin doctrīna.

s.def: a social event were beverages are served, with or without snacks, e.g. as a celebration
a recreational drug, psychoactive substance, especially when illegal and addictive
drone (non-working male bee, ant or wasp)

dar.e: Inherited from Middle Dutch darne, by metathesis from dorne, from Old Dutch *drān, from Proto-West Germanic *drānu, from Proto-Germanic *drēnuz. Doublet of drone.

duel.e: Borrowed from Middle French duel, from Latin duellum (“duel; war”), archaic form of bellum (“war”). In Mediaeval Latin the meaning shifted from “war” to “duel” because of folk etymology associating it with duo (“two”).

dollar.e: Borrowed from English dollar, from early Dutch daler, daalder.

diesel.e: Backformed from early compounds like dieselmotor. Named after Rudolf Diesel.

s.def: dell, small depression in a landscape

del.e: From Middle Dutch delle (“valley; dune valley”), from Old Dutch della (“valley; (geography) depression; dune valley”), from Proto-Germanic *daljō. Cognate with English dell.

examples: Maa ziet gij da dan ni, 't ligt veur ave neus, gij sen deem!

s.def: related to the dada art movement

examples: Zeg dada aan oma.
Oma gaat dada.
Wil je dodo doen?

s.def: pronominal adverb form of dat; that
examples: Daar ben ik blij om.

s.def: since (preceding a reason why something is like it is)
examples: Daar ze geen zin heeft, komt ze niet

daar.e: From Middle Dutch dâer, from Old Dutch thār, dār, from Proto-West Germanic *þār.

drank.e: From Middle Dutch dranc, from Old Dutch *drank, from Proto-Germanic *drankiz, ultimately from Proto-Indo-European *dʰrenǵ- (“to pull; draw; sip”); compare German Trank (“drink; potion”).

dupe.e: Borrowed from French dupe, from Middle French [Term?].

examples: Hij schoot de bal door het raam.
Dolenthousiast rende het hondje door de kamer.
Door files kan ik niet op tijd komen.
Hij vermeed een confrontatie door de andere kant op te lopen.
Ondanks slecht weer ging het feest toch door.
Ik rijd nu de stad door.
Dolenthousiast rende het hondje de kamer door.

door.e: From Middle Dutch dōre, from Old Dutch thuro, from Proto-Germanic *þurhw.

examples: Sy roept, du bist een slaef, in mijne dienst gebonden
Soo leyt dyn memmetje dy in dyn wiechje te rust.

du.e: From Middle Dutch du, from Old Dutch thū, from Proto-West Germanic *þū, from Proto-Germanic *þū, from Proto-Indo-European *túh₂.

s.def: doctor (person who has attained a doctorate)

doctor.e: From Middle Dutch doctor, from Latin doctor (“teacher, instructor”).

s.def: The fourth letter of the Dutch alphabet, written in the Latin script.
The fourth letter of the Dutch alphabet, written in the Latin script.

dank.e: From Middle Dutch danc, from Old Dutch thank, from Proto-Germanic *þankaz.

damp.e: From Middle Dutch damp, from Old Dutch *damp, from Proto-Germanic *dampaz.

s.def: to steam, to give off steam or smoke
to vape (to inhale the vapour of an electronic cigarette)

dampen.e: From damp + -en. The meaning “to vape” is a semantic loan from English.

discreet.e: From Middle Dutch discreet, from Old French discret, from Medieval Latin discrētus, from discernō.

dame.e: From Middle Dutch dame, from Middle French dame, from Old French dame, from Latin domina.

duo.e: From French duo or Italian duo, from Latin duo (“two”).

s.def: a river delta, a network of one or more rivers and tributaries pouring out into a larger body of water

delta.e: Ultimately from Ancient Greek δέλτα (délta). Doublet of dalet.

decameter.e: Borrowed from French décamètre. Equivalent to deca- + meter.

s.def: a dino, a dinosaur; archosaur of the super-order Dinosauria

dino.e: Likely borrowed from English dino. Equivalent to a clipping of dinosaurus. The term became especially prominent after the release of Jurassic Park in 1993, but predated the film by at least one or two years.

s.def: decimetre: one tenth of a metre

decimeter.e: Borrowed from French décimètre. Equivalent to deci- + meter.

examples: We gaan over twee dagen op vakantie.
Een dag duurt 24 uur.
De dierentuin is geopend tijdens de dag, zodat bezoekers de dieren in hun natuurlijke omgeving kunnen zien.
We genieten van de zon en het strand tijdens de dag.

s.def: a meeting or assembly with legal or political power, originally convened on a specific day; a diet
hello, short for goedendag (“good day”) 'goodday; goodbye'
a piece of rope, used to punish sailors with, on the spot or in running the gauntlet
a line used to fasten young sailors while training boarding a hostile ship or climbing the rigging

dag.e: From Middle Dutch dach, from Old Dutch dag, from Proto-West Germanic *dag, from Proto-Germanic *dagaz.

s.def: a hamlet in Gennep, Limburg, Netherlands
a hamlet in Hollands Kroon, North Holland, Netherlands

dam.e: From Middle Dutch dam, from Old Dutch dam, from Proto-West Germanic *damm, from Proto-Germanic *dammaz.

s.def: a type of stone to pave the floor with, flagstone
a hamlet in Gulpen-Wittem, Limburg, Netherlands

dal.e: From Middle Dutch dal, from Old Dutch dal, from Proto-West Germanic *dal, from Proto-Germanic *dalą.

decibel.e: Borrowed from English decibel. Equivalent to deci- + bel.

dubnium.e: Borrowed from English dubnium or Russian ду́бний (dúbnij). Named after Dubna in Russia.

deuterium.e: Borrowed from English deuterium. Coined by Harold Urey, an American chemist, from Ancient Greek δεύτερος (deúteros, “second”) + -ium.

decigram.e: Borrowed from French décigramme. Equivalent to deci- + gram.

examples: Hier komen drie wegen samen.
De genodigden arriveerden iets voor drieën.

s.def: a hamlet in Ermelo, Gelderland, Netherlands

drie.e: From Middle Dutch drie, from Old Dutch thrie, from Proto-West Germanic *þrīʀ, from Proto-Germanic *þrīz, from Proto-Indo-European *tréyes.

dertien.e: From Middle Dutch dertene, dertiene, from Old Dutch *thritēn, *thritien, from Proto-Germanic *þritehun. Compare West Frisian trettjin, German dreizehn, English thirteen, Danish tretten.

decagram.e: Borrowed from French décagramme. Equivalent to deca- + gram.

s.def: genitive singular feminine of de; of the
examples: geschiedenis der Nederlandsche taal

s.def: genitive plural of de; of the
examples: het koninkrijk der Nederlanden

s.def: A badger, various species of genera Meles and Taxidea.

das.e: From Middle Dutch das, from Old Dutch *thas, from Proto-West Germanic *þahs, from Proto-Germanic *þahsuz.

dok.e:
From Middle Dutch docke (“port, harbour, roadstead”), of uncertain origin. The original sense may have been "the furrow a grounded vessel makes in a mud bank". Compare Middle Low German docke (“dock”), borrowed from the Middle Dutch.
Some sources link this word to an unattested Middle Dutch *dock...

daniel.e: The village is named after a former plantation house.

s.def: dharma (order of the universe, society, etc.; one's social or religious duty in Hinduism; teachings of the Buddha)

drone.e: Borrowed from English drone (“aircraft drone”). Doublet with dar (“male bee”), which descended from Middle Dutch, cf. Limburgish dreen.

deca-.e: Borrowed from French déca-, from Late Latin decas (“(set of) ten”), from Ancient Greek δεκάς (dekás), from δέκα (déka, “ten”).

deci-.e: Borrowed from French déci-, from Latin decimus (“tenth”).

s.def: drama (theatrical work; anything involving play acting)

drama.e: Borrowed from Late Latin drāma, from Ancient Greek δρᾶμα (drâma).

s.def: down, small bird feathers used as insulation material in covers and sleeping bags
a bed cover (e.g. a duvet) filled with down
a piece of down or fluff

dons.e: From Middle Dutch donst or Middle Low German dūne, all from Old Norse dúnn.

dis.e: From Middle Dutch disch, from Old Dutch disk, from Proto-Germanic *diskuz (“table; dish; bowl”), from Latin discus. Cognate with English dish and German Tisch (“table”).

dans.e: From Middle Dutch dans, from Old French danse or a deverbal from dansen.

examples: Het Duitsch wordt verdeeld in Hoogduitsch en Nederduitsch. Het groot verschil tusschen beide is de tweede of hoogduitsche klankverschuiving ([...]), waarvan het Nederduitsch vrij gebleven is. [...] Het Nederduitsch begrijpt het Saksisch en het Nederfrankisch. In het Oudsaksisch bestaat [...] heden heet zij Nedersaksisch, of Nederduitsch in eenen engeren zin, of ook nog, en meest Platduitsch.

Vanaf de tweede helft van de zestiende eeuw komt ook het Nederduits sterk opzetten, als tegenhanger van Hoogduits [...]. Nederduits omvat dus zowel het Nederlands als het Duits dat in Noord-Duitsland wordt gesproken.

duits.e: From Middle Dutch duutsch, a Northern variant of dietsch, both from Old Dutch *thiudisc, from Proto-West Germanic *þiudisk, from Proto-Germanic *þiudiskaz, from *þeudō (“people, nation”), from Proto-Indo-European *tewtéh₂ (“people”). Doublet of Diets.

s.def: Drenthe, a province of the Netherlands

drenthe.e: From Middle Dutch [Term?], from late Old Dutch Drente, from earlier Old Dutch Thrente, from Old Dutch Thriente, from Old Dutch Thrianta (also Old Dutch Threant), from *thrie (“three”) + *hant-. Beware that these attestations come to us via copies of Latin texts, and may therefore be affected by t...

datum.e: From Middle Dutch datum, from Latin datum (“given”, past participle) (from the practice of signing letters in Latin by noting the date on which they were dispatched). Compare English date.

direct.e: Borrowed from Middle French direct, from Latin dīrēctus.

s.def: tarragon (the edible Mediterranean herb Artemisia dracunculus)

dragon.e: Borrowed from Middle French dragon, from Arabic طَرْخُون (ṭarḵūn), from Ancient Greek δρακόντιον (drakóntion).

s.def: a festival of song and dance organised and performed by and for enslaved people
sleepy, dozy, not fully awake or to one's senses

does.e: Borrowed from West Frisian dûs, ultimately related to Proto-West Germanic *dwās (“stupid”).

detail.e: Borrowed from French détail, from Middle French [Term?], from Old French detail, from detaillier, from de- + taillier (“to cut”).

examples: Nederland in den goeden ouden tijd.
De baron gaf den koetsier een wenk en het rijtuig rolde heen.

den.e: From Middle Dutch dan, danne, denne (“pine tree”), from Old Dutch *danna, from Proto-West Germanic *dannā (“pine tree”). Related to German Tanne.

dik.e: From Middle Dutch dicke, from Old Dutch *thicki, from Proto-West Germanic *þikkwī, from Proto-Germanic *þekuz, from Proto-Indo-European *tégus.

s.def: a duomo, either an episcopal cathedral or another major church (often a basilica) which has been granted this high rank
an ecclesiastical form of address, notably for a Benedictine priest
a nobleman or clergyman in certain Catholic countries, notably Portugal and its colonies

dom.e: From Middle Dutch dom, domp, from Old Dutch dump, from Proto-West Germanic *dumb, from Proto-Germanic *dumbaz.

examples: De duurste Stradivarius is 'The Hammer' die in 2006 $3,5 miljoen opleverde.

s.def: a hamlet in Olst-Wijhe, Overijssel, Netherlands

duur.e: From Middle Dutch diere, dure, from Old Dutch diuri, from Proto-Germanic *diurijaz. Doublet of dier.

s.def: a silly person, usually referring to a woman; a silly goose

demon.e: From Latin daemon (“lar, genius, guardian spirit”), from Ancient Greek δαίμων (daímōn, “dispenser, god, protective spirit”).

s.def: a doit, a Dutch copper coin with the value of ¹⁄₁₆₀ of a guilder, current before the decimalization of 1816
an amount of money, a sum of money
examples: Zij kon daarmee een aardige duit verdienen.
"Blijf met je gore klauwen van me duiten af", krijste de vrek.

duit.e: From Middle Dutch duit, of uncertain origin. Possibly borrowed from Old Norse þveit (“cut-off piece of metal, small coin, doit”), related to Old English þwītan (“to cut, cutt off”) (whence dialectal English thwite).

s.def: then, at that time (in the future)
examples: Morgen wordt het beter weer, maar dan moet ik weer naar mijn werk.
Eerst moet je je tanden poetsen, dan mag je naar bed.
Als het niet had geregend of gesneeuwd had, dan moet de auto toch veilig zijn.
Ik ben ouder dan jij.
Niets dan liefde.

s.def: unit of grading proficiency of black belt or greater than black-belt in Japanese martial arts

dan.e: From Middle Dutch dan, from Old Dutch than, from Proto-West Germanic *þan, from Proto-Germanic *þan.

s.def: licorice, especially a variety sold as small sweets/candies

drop.e: From Middle Dutch drope (“drop”), from Old Dutch dropo, from Proto-Germanic *drupô. When unsuffixed, drop is more common than drup, but suffixed druppel is more common than droppel; this is likely due to the effects of i-umlaut triggered by the suffix -el. Indeed, drup is assumed to be based on d...

dessert.e: Borrowed from French dessert, from desservir (“disserve”), from dés- (“dis-”) and servir (“serve”), thus literally meaning “removal of what has been served”.

examples: Ze opende de deur en stapte de kamer binnen.
De deur van het huis was blauw.

deur.e: From Middle Dutch dōre, duere, from Old Dutch duri, from Proto-West Germanic *dur, from Proto-Germanic *durz, from Proto-Indo-European *dʰwer- (“doorway, door, gate”).

s.def: animal (any member of the kingdom Animalia)
her, their, the latter's (genitive feminine singular and genitive plural of die)
examples: De verdachte heeft zich samen met een vriend, haar dochter en dier vriend schuldig gemaakt aan de moord op haar echtgenoot … (from a verdict of the Court of Justice at 's-Gravenhage, 2011 http://jure.nl/bq6330)
The accused (woman) is guilty of having murdered her husband in cooperation with a friend, her daughter and the latter's friend …

dier.e: From Middle Dutch dier, from Old Dutch dier, from Proto-West Germanic *deuʀ, from Proto-Germanic *deuzą, from Proto-Indo-European *dʰewsóm.

s.def: garb, clothing, especially extravagant or unusual clothes
patch of hair, especially one's headhair

examples: dioxide
dipool

s.def: the combined Flemish, Dutch and Afrikaner people

diet.e: Revival by Flemish nationalists of Middle Dutch diet (“people, folk”), from Proto-West Germanic *þeudu, from Proto-Germanic *þeudō, from Proto-Indo-European *tewtéh₂. Compare Diets (“Dutch, German”).

s.def: deva, a Hindu or Buddhist deity
a hamlet in Aalten, Gelderland, Netherlands

dale.e: First attested as dale in 1386. Dative plural of dal (“dale, valley”).

s.def: a duet (musical piece performed by two players or two singers)
a ballet routine performed by two dancers

duet.e: Borrowed from Italian duetto or German Duett (itself borrowed from Italian).

diner.e: Borrowed from French dîner, from Middle French [Term?], from Old French disner.

s.def: a male given name from Hebrew, equivalent to English David
a surname originating as a patronymic

david.e: Ultimately from Late Latin Dāvīd, from Koine Greek Δαυῑ̈́δ (Dauī̈́d), from Biblical Hebrew דָּוִד (dāwiḏ).

examples: Hij was des bewust te dien tijde.

s.def: the ... the (used with te as an intensifier to indicate the degree of an action)
examples: Des te vaker de mensen Willem de rug toekeren des te beter!

s.def: a tuft, a bunch, a clump

examples: een dot geld

s.def: darling, sweetie (almost always used in its diminutive form - dotje)

dot.e: A secondary form of dodde (“long, soft raceme, as of a cattail plant”), a word of uncertain but perhaps gibberish origin related to German Dutte, düttenkolb (“bulrush”), themselves linked to words like Zitze, Titte.

diameter.e: Borrowed from French diamètre, from Latin diametrus, from Ancient Greek διάμετρος (diámetros). Equivalent to dia- + meter.

disco.e: Borrowed from English disco. Equivalent to a shortening of discotheek.

s.def: dingo, Canis lupus dingo (Australian wild dog)

dingo.e: Borrowed from English dingo, from Dharug dingu.

dapper.e: From Middle Dutch dapper, from Old Dutch *dappar, from Proto-Germanic *dapraz.

examples: Doe dat daar maar in.

s.def: to cause to, to make; forms causative verbs
examples: Dat doet mij denken aan vorig jaar.
Doe mij maar een baco.

s.def: to do, to have sex with someone
examples: Zou je haar doen?

s.def: to touch a nerve; to strike a chord in
examples: Het doet me wat.

s.def: to be different; to make a difference
examples: De beide camera's doen elkaar niet veel (onder).

s.def: to behave or act in some way
examples: Doe maar gewoon.

doen.e: From Middle Dutch doen, from Old Dutch duon, from Proto-West Germanic *dōn, from Proto-Germanic *dōną, from Proto-Indo-European *dʰéh₁t.

s.def: a diminutive of the male given name Diederik

dancing.e: Pseudo-anglicism, derived from dancing; compare Italian, Spanish and French dancing.

s.def: Delft (a city and municipality of South Holland, Netherlands)

delft.e: First attested as delf in 1130-1157. From Middle Dutch delft, from earlier delf, derived from a hydronym derived from Middle Dutch delven (“dig, excavate”).

s.def: a delver, a digger, an excavator

delver.e: From Middle Dutch delvere. Equivalent to delven + -er.

s.def: dogger, fishing boat used for catching cod and haddock
cod and haddock fisherman who works from a fishing boat
tubular net or fish trap used for catching cod and haddock
Dogger, area near the Dogger Bank
Dogger, Middle Jurassic, second period of the Jurassic
dry, wilted (having a relatively low or no liquid content)

dor.e: From Middle Dutch dorre, from Old Dutch *thurri, from Proto-West Germanic *þurʀī, from Proto-Germanic *þursuz, from Proto-Indo-European *ters-.

duffel.e: From the Belgian town of Duffel.

s.def: a hamlet in Hilvarenbeek, North Brabant, Netherlands

dun.e: From Middle Dutch dunne, from Old Dutch *thunni, from Proto-West Germanic *þunnī, from Proto-Germanic *þunnuz. Cognate with English thin (Compare West-Flemish thinne).

dynamo.e: Borrowed from English dynamo, from German dynamoelektrische Maschine.

diabetes.e: From New Latin diabetes mellitus, from Ancient Greek διαβαίνω (diabaínō, “to pass through”), via the agent noun διαβήτης (diabḗtēs, “passing through”). This refers to the excessive amounts of urine produced by sufferers.

document.e: Borrowed from Middle French document, from Latin documentum.

dominant.e: Borrowed from French dominant, from Middle French dominant.

examples: Ik heb als douceurtje bij de deal ook maar een fles goede wijn gegeven.

douche.e: Borrowed from French douche (“shower”), from Italian doccia (“shower”). See also does (“shower head”).

drab.e:
Uncertain, but probably related to Dutch draf (“dregs”) (from Proto-Germanic *drabaz). Compare Low German drabbe (“silt”).
First attested as Dutch drabbe (“sediment”) in 1599.

s.def: strong and sudden upwelling of anger: a fit
deviation of direction caused by wind: drift
path along which cattle are driven

drift.e: From Middle Dutch drift, also dricht, from Old Dutch *drift, from Proto-West Germanic *drifti, from Proto-Germanic *driftiz. Equivalent to drijven (“to drive”) + -t (“verbal noun”).

examples: Zij heeft geen hoge dunk van de nieuwe soep.
De Amerikaanse veteraan was nog steeds verbolgen over de donuts van het Rode Kruis.

donut.e: Borrowed from English donut, from older doughnut.

diagram.e: Borrowed from French diagramme or English diagram, from Latin diagramma, from Ancient Greek διάγραμμα (diágramma).

drinken.e: From Middle Dutch drinken, from Old Dutch drincan, from Proto-West Germanic *drinkan, from Proto-Germanic *drinkaną.

duwen.e: From Middle Dutch duwen, douwen, from Old Dutch thuwen, from Proto-West Germanic *þų̄hijan, from Proto-Germanic *þunhijaną.

duiken.e: From Middle Dutch duken, from Old Dutch *dūcan, from Proto-West Germanic *dūkan, from Proto-Germanic *dūkaną. Cognate with English (to) duck.

dobber.e: From Middle Dutch dobber, of unclear further origin.

diagnose.e: Borrowed from French diagnose. Ultimately from Latin diagnōsis, from Ancient Greek διάγνωσις (diágnōsis), from διαγιγνώσκω (diagignṓskō, “to discern”), from διά (diá, “through”) + γιγνώσκω (gignṓskō, “to know”).

examples: De vogel lag dood op de grond.

dood.e: From Middle Dutch dôot, doet, from Old Dutch dōt, from Proto-West Germanic *daud, from Proto-Germanic *daudaz. Compare West Frisian dead, German tot, English dead, Danish død.

dealer.e: From English dealer. By surface analysis, dealen (“to deal drugs”) + -er. Doublet of deler.

district.e: From Middle Dutch district, from Middle French district, from Medieval Latin districtus (“a district within which the lord may distrain, also jurisdiction”), from Latin districtus, past participle of distringō, distringere (“draw asunder, compel, distrain”), from dis- (“apart”) + stringō, stringe...

discipline.e: From Middle Dutch discipline, from Old French discipline, from Latin disciplīna.

déjà vu.e: Unadapted borrowing from French déjà vu.

s.def: a minor depression, a short-lived sadness
a minor economic setback, no worse than a short, minor recession
drum, usually one belonging to a drum kit
dahlia, plant of the genus Dahlia

dilemma.e: Borrowed from Latin dilemma, from Ancient Greek δίλημμα (dílēmma).

s.def: director, manager (in a company), general manager, CEO (or at a lower (e.g., branch) level)
director (a person in charge of various other institutions)
director or manager (who has an executive mandate within an institution, e.g., for HR)

drek.e:
From Middle Dutch drec, from Old Dutch *threkk, from Proto-West Germanic *þraki, from Proto-Germanic *þrakjaz, from Proto-Indo-European *(s)terǵ-, *(s)terḱ-, *(s)treḱ- (“manure, dung; to sully, soil, decay”).
Compare English dreck, German Dreck (“dirt; filth”), Old Norse þrekkr, Swedish träck. Wi...

s.def: deed, official document entitling one to something

diploma.e: Borrowed from Latin diplōma, from Ancient Greek δίπλωμα (díplōma). The sense “diploma” derived from French diplôme.

s.def: dogma (colloquially with connotations of strictness and inflexibility)

dogma.e: Borrowed from Latin dogma, from Ancient Greek δόγμα (dógma).

s.def: a hamlet in Westerkwartier, Groningen, Netherlands
a neighbourhood of Zoetermeer, South Holland, Netherlands

dorp.e: From Middle Dutch dorp, from Old Dutch thorp, from Proto-West Germanic *þorp, from Proto-Germanic *þurpą. Doublet of terp.

s.def: a diorama (three-dimensional display of a setting or scenery)
examples: De poëzij, deze wettige koningin van de tooneelkunst, heeft er thans slechts den tweeden rang; men doet er heerschen het schilderwerk, de beweging der schermen, zonder te denken, dat deze soort van begoocheling hier nooit eene volkomene uitwerking zal hebben, terwijl de diorama's en panorama's altijd de gelukkigste pogingen van den werktuigkunstenaar en tooneelschilder overtreffen.

diorama.e: Borrowed from French diorama, coined by Louis Daguerre.

dansen.e: From Middle Dutch dansen, from Old French danc(i)er.

dit.e: From Middle Dutch dit, from Old Dutch thit. Cognate with German dies.

From Middle Dutch diamant, from Old French diamant, from Medieval Latin and Late Latin diamas, variant of Latin adamas.
The typographic use derives from Dirck Voskens, who first cut it around 1700 and presumably named it by analogy with the larger parel.

december.e: From Middle Dutch december, from Latin December.

examples: Ik heb een doktersafspraak op donderdag.

donderdag.e:
From Middle Dutch donresdach, donredach, from Old Dutch *thonaresdag, from Proto-West Germanic *Þunras dag (“day of the thunder god”). Equivalent to Donar or donder + dag.
Donar (North Germanic: Thor) was the Germanic god of thunder.

examples: We hebben op dinsdag een vergadering.
De dinsdagen zijn altijd mijn langste dagen op werk.

dinsdag.e:
From Middle Dutch dinsendach. Probably from Proto-Germanic *þingsus-dagaz (“Day of Thingsus”), from *þingsus + *dagaz. Thingsus, Tiw or Týr was the name of the Germanic god of justice.
Compare Low German Dingsdag and German Dienstag.

examples: Vette diss, man!

dengue.e: Borrowed from Spanish dengue or indirectly via French dengue (itself from Spanish), probably from Swahili dinga.

detective.e: Borrowed from English detective. The sense "work of detective fiction" comes from an ellipsis of detective story.

dissident.e: Borrowed from French dissident, from Latin dissidens, present participle of dissidere (“to sit apart, to disagree”); dis- + sedere (“to sit”).

examples: 'O, we hebben net seks gehad in die dixi daar.' Hij wijst met een stalen gezicht naar een oranje wc-hokje, achter een heleboel hekken die het festival van het backstagegebied scheiden.

donor.e: Borrowed from English donor, from Middle English donoure, from Old French doneur.

dong.e: From Middle Dutch *dong, from Old Dutch *dunga, from Proto-Germanic *dungō. Cognate to English dung.

diastole.e: Borrowed from French diastole, from Ancient Greek διαστολή (diastolḗ, “separation, drawing asunder”).

examples: Dit dijbeen is daarom merkwaardig, omdat 't het bestaan eener derde soort „Dinosaurus” in de formatie bij Maastricht doet vermoeden.
Nabij Wyoming in de Vereenigde Staten heeft men het grootste voorwereldlijke dier ontdekt, dat bekend is, namelijk den brontosaurus, een kruipend dier, behoorende tot de orde der dinosaurussen.

dinosaurus.e: Borrowed from New Latin dinosaurus, from English dinosaur.

dealen.e: Borrowed from English deal. By surface analysis, deal + -en.

examples: Ik denk vaak aan mijn vriendin.

denken.e: From Middle Dutch denken, from Old Dutch thenken, from Proto-West Germanic *þankijan, from Proto-Germanic *þankijaną, from Proto-Indo-European *teng-.

debutante.e: Borrowed from French débutante or from debutant + -e.

dagblad.e: From dag + blad. Compare southern German Tagblatt.

docent.e: From Latin docēns, present participle of doceō (“to teach”).

dé.e: An emphatically stressed variant of the definite article de.

delict.e: From Middle Dutch delict, from Latin dēlīctum (“fault”), neuter of dēlīctus, past participle of delinquo (“to fail; to be lacking”).

darter.e: Borrowed from English darter. By surface analysis, darten + -er.

dineren.e: Borrowed from French dîner, from Middle French [Term?], from Old French disner.

dragen.e: From Middle Dutch drāgen, from Old Dutch dragan, from Proto-Germanic *draganą (“draw, pull, carry”).

dagen.e: From Middle Dutch dāgen, from Old Dutch *dagon, from Proto-West Germanic *dagēn, from Proto-Germanic *dagāną.

examples: divergente reeks

divergent.e: Borrowed from Latin dīvergēns, the active present participle of the Latin verb that also underlies the borrowed Dutch divergeren.

examples: Waarom zijn al die dingen daar ineens maar neergezet? / Is er een dingenmotie aangenomen, of een dingenwet? / Dat in elke straat en elke laan voortaan een soort van ding moet staan? / Ik liep laatst over straat en ik zag 'n ding staan

s.def: thing (popular assembly or judicial council in early Germanic society)

ding.e: From Middle Dutch dinc, from Old Dutch thinc, from Proto-West Germanic *þing, from Proto-Germanic *þingą.

daven.e: From Middle Dutch *daven, further etymology uncertain. May be related to Old Saxon dovon, Old High German tobēn, but the vowel a is irregular in this case. It is found in Middle Low German daven, where it may be regular, which makes it a possible Middle Low German borrowing.

dammen.e: Borrowed from Middle French dame (“dame, double checkers piece”) + -en.

depot.e: Either from Middle Dutch depoost, from Middle French deposte, from Latin dēpositus, with adaptation of the spelling and pronunciation to Modern French dépôt, or borrowed anew from French dépôt, from the same Middle French word.

decaliter.e: Borrowed from French décalitre. Equivalent to deca- + liter.

drom.e: From Middle Dutch droom, from Proto-Germanic *þrumi, *dramjan, related to *þrumjaz (“disturbance, violence”). See also Old Saxon drom, Old English þrymm.

dak.e: From Middle Dutch dac, from Old Dutch thak, from Proto-West Germanic *þak, from Proto-Germanic *þaką, from Proto-Indo-European *teg-.

doos.e: From Middle Dutch dose (since 1361), probably from Latin dosis (“prescribed portion of medication”), metonymically transferred to the small box in which a dose of medication was given.

s.def: lid (top or cover of a container)

deksel.e: From Middle Dutch decsel. Equivalent to dekken (“to cover”) + -sel.

drogen.e: From Middle Dutch drôgen. Equivalent to droog + -en.

examples: Melk dient gekoeld bewaard te blijven.

dienen.e: From Middle Dutch dienen, from Old Dutch thienon, from Proto-Germanic *þewanōną.

drijven.e: From Middle Dutch driven, from Old Dutch drīvan, from Proto-West Germanic *drīban, from Proto-Germanic *drībaną, from Proto-Indo-European *dʰreybʰ- (“to drive, push”).

dromen.e: From Middle Dutch drômen, from Old Dutch *drōmen, from Proto-West Germanic *draumijan, from Proto-Germanic *draumijaną.

delen.e: From Middle Dutch dêlen, from Old Dutch dēlen, from Proto-Germanic *dailijaną. Cognate with English deal.

doorzichtig.e: From Middle Dutch doresichtich. Equivalent to doorzicht + -ig.

donker.e: From Middle Dutch donker, dunker, from Old Dutch *dunkar, from Proto-Germanic *dunkalaz, *dunkaraz.

diep.e:
From Middle Dutch diep, from Old Dutch diep, from Proto-West Germanic *deup, from Proto-Germanic *deupaz, from Proto-Indo-European *dʰewb-.
Compare Low German diep, deep, West Frisian djip, English deep, German tief, Danish dyb, Norwegian dyp.

dochter.e: From Middle Dutch dochter, from Old Dutch dohter, from Proto-West Germanic *dohter, from Proto-Germanic *duhtēr, from Proto-Indo-European *dʰugh₂tḗr.

dus.e: From Middle Dutch dos, dus, from Old Dutch thus, from Proto-West Germanic *þus.

draad.e: From Middle Dutch drâet, from Old Dutch *thrād, from Proto-Germanic *þrēduz. Equivalent to draaien (“to turn”) + -d (“verbal noun”).

delicatessen.e: First attested 1642 http://www.etymologiebank.nl/trefwoord/delicatesse. Plural of delicatesse (“fine food”), from French délicatesse, from délicat (“fine”), from Latin delicatus (“alluring”).

denemarken.e:
First attested as Denemarcket, Denemarckt and Denemarken in the 16th century. Potentially a compound of Dutch Low Saxon deen (“rough terrain”) and Old Frisian merke (“boundary, march, undivided communal lands”).
See also Dutch Low Saxon Denmaark.

donatie.e: Borrowed from Middle French donation, from Latin dōnātiō.

dokter.e: From doctor, assimilated to Dutch phonetics through common use, from Middle Dutch doctoor, from Latin doctor.

deugd.e: From Middle Dutch dōghet, dōget, from Old Dutch *dugath, *dugeth, *dugith (“virtue”), from Proto-Germanic *dugunþō (“usefulness, virtue”), from Proto-Indo-European *dʰewgʰ- (“to be ready, be sufficient”).

dagelijks.e: From Middle Dutch dagelics, from Old Dutch dagawelikis, a compound of dag (“day”) + the genitive of welik (“which, whichever, any”) (modern welk). Equivalent to dag + -e- + -lijks.

discus.e: Borrowed from Latin discus, from Ancient Greek δίσκος (dískos). First attested in the eighteenth century.

diligence.e: Borrowed from French diligence, from Latin diligentia.

dimmer.e: Borrowed from English dimmer. Equivalent to dimmen + -er.

doek.e: From Middle Dutch doec, from Old Dutch *duok, from Proto-West Germanic *dōk, from Proto-Germanic *dōkaz.

decor.e: Borrowed from French décor, from Latin decor or decus.

den haag.e: From Middle Dutch Die Hāge (“the enclosure”), from hāge (m, later also f, “enclosure, hedge”). First attested in 1242 as die haga. The article form den is dative/accusative, generalized in the modern name because of its use after prepositions, e.g. in den Haghe (“in The Hague”). Compare Den Bosch...

dertig.e: From Middle Dutch dertich, from Old Dutch *thrītig, from Proto-Germanic *þrīz tigiwiz.

damar.e: Borrowed from Malay damar, from Proto-Malayic *damar, from Proto-Malayo-Polynesian *damaʀ.

From Middle Dutch dwerch, from Old Dutch *dwerg, from Proto-West Germanic *dwerg, from Proto-Germanic *dwergaz.
Compare West Frisian dwerch, German Zwerg, English dwarf, Danish dværg.

dubbeltje.e: Already attested in the 17th century. From dubbel (“double”) + -tje (diminutive). Named for having twice the value of a stuiver.

druipen.e: From Middle Dutch drupen, from Old Dutch driepan, *drūpan, from Proto-West Germanic *dreupan, from Proto-Germanic *dreupaną.

dooien.e: From Middle Dutch dôyen, from Old Dutch *thōien, from Proto-West Germanic *þauwjan, from Proto-Germanic *þawjaną.

doden.e: From Middle Dutch dôden, from Old Dutch *dōden, from Proto-West Germanic *daudijan, from Proto-Germanic *daudijaną. By surface analysis, dood (“dead”) + -en. Compare German töten and English deaden.

dopen.e: From Middle Dutch dôpen, from Old Dutch dōpen, from Proto-West Germanic *daupijan, from Proto-Germanic *daupijaną.

doof.e: From Middle Dutch dôof, from Old Dutch dōf, from Proto-West Germanic *daub, from Proto-Germanic *daubaz, from Proto-Indo-European *dʰewbʰ- (“to whisk, be obscured”).

dij.e:
From Middle Dutch die, from Old Dutch *thio, from Proto-West Germanic *þeuh, from Proto-Germanic *þeuhą. Compare West Frisian tsjea, English thigh, Icelandic þjó.
The expected modern form would be *die. The diphthongised form may have originated in the plural, where Middle Dutch dien could easily...

examples: Wil jij de deur dichtdoen?

danken.e: From Middle Dutch danken, from Old Dutch thancon, from Proto-West Germanic *þankōn, from Proto-Germanic *þankōną. Equivalent to dank + -en.

dulden.e: From Middle Dutch dulden, from Old Dutch tholon, from Proto-Germanic *þulāną, from Proto-Indo-European *telh₂-.

drog.e: From Middle Dutch droch, ultimately from the second element of bedriegen (“to deceive”).

doppio.e: Borrowed from Italian doppio. Doublet of dubbel, duplo, and double.

duodecimo.e: Borrowed from Latin in duodecimō (“in a twelfth”), from duodecimus (“twelfth”).

dringen.e: From Middle Dutch dringen, from Old Dutch *thringan, from Proto-West Germanic *þringwan, from Proto-Germanic *þrinhwaną.

dante.e: Attested as Dante and Op-de-Dante in 1936. Etymology unknown. Not derived from Middle Dutch dan (“woodland vale, animal lair”) or Middle Dutch dante (“floozie”).

examples: We stonden voor een dichte deur.
Ik spring lachend in het diepe met m'n ogen dicht.

dicht.e: From Middle Dutch dichte, from Old Dutch *thīhti, from Proto-West Germanic *þį̄ht(ī), from Proto-Germanic *þinhtaz. Cognate with English tight and German dicht (“dense”).

examples: De verdachte beweerde dat hij handelde onder dwang en bedreiging.
Het gebruik van dwang wordt algemeen veroordeeld in de samenleving.

dwang.e: From Middle Dutch dwanc, from Old Dutch *thwang, from Proto-West Germanic *þwangi, from Proto-Germanic *þwangiz.

druif.e: From Middle Dutch druve, from Old Dutch thrūva, from Proto-West Germanic *þrūbō (“grape”).

examples: De droogte heeft de oogst dit jaar sterk beïnvloed.
Er zijn maatregelen genomen vanwege de aanhoudende droogte.

droogte.e: From Middle Dutch droochte. Equivalent to droog + -te.

s.def: a falling drop of liquid or its dried-up result

dolen.e: From Middle Dutch dolen, from Old Dutch *dolon. Equivalent to dol + -en, next to newer dollen.

daphne.e: Ultimately from Ancient Greek δάφνη (dáphnē, “laurel, bay”), perhaps via French or alternatively English.

doper.e: From Middle Dutch dôpere, from Old Dutch dōperi. Equivalent to dopen + -er.

* durren (“to dare”), from Old Dutch durran, from Proto-West Germanic *durʀan, from Proto-Germanic *durzaną.

s.def: a dayworker, day-labourer; one who works for daily wages, and is employed on daily basis, without mid- or long-term job security
a speech of thanks, a word of thanks
a dalmatic, notably as Catholic liturgic vestment

dalmatiek.e: From Middle Dutch dalmatike, from Ecclesiastical Latin dalmatica, from the feminine of Dalmaticus (“Dalmatian”), from Dalmatia; cognate with French dalmatique, German Dalmatika etc.

s.def: Dalmatia (a historical region of Croatia, on the eastern coast of the Adriatic Sea)
Dalmatia (a province of the Roman Empire)

dalmatië.e: From Middle Dutch dalmatien, from Latin Dalmatia.

s.def: Dalmatian, from or relating to Dalmatia

dalmatisch.e: From Dalmatië + -isch. Cf. Latin Dalmaticus, from Dalmatia.

s.def: the third estate, the commoners as a represented (mainly bourgeoisie and/or cities as such) feudal estate below the privileged nobility and clergy; often excluding an even lowlier fourth estate, which is denied representation
to dock, to place in a dock
to dock, to go into a dock

drinkrietje.e: Compound of drinken (“to drink”) + rietje (“drinking straw”).

s.def: a damask rose (Rosa × damascena), a source of attar and major rose breeding ancestor, notably of hybrid perpetual roses
a table companion, one of several people seated and eating at the same dinner table

disgenoot.e: From dis (“(set) table; meal”) + genoot (“fellow, companion”).

dankbaarheid.e: From Middle Dutch dancbaerheit. Equivalent to dankbaar (“grateful”) + -heid.

s.def: a tripod: vessel, stool, bench or stand on three legs

doodsoorzaak.e: From dood (“death”) + -s- + oorzaak (“cause”). Compare German Todesursache.

s.def: a death's-head, a (human) skull, notably as an emblem
thankless, ungrateful, yielding (little or) no gratitude or reward
examples: Belastingontvanger en beul zijn dankloze beroepen.

s.def: a tierce, third or triad, spanning three tones in an octave
a triphthong (a monosyllabic vowel combination usually involving a quick but smooth movement from one vowel to another that passes over a third one)

drieklank.e: Compound of drie (“three”) + klank (“sound”). The phonetic sense is a semantic loan from Ancient Greek τρίφθογγος (tríphthongos).

s.def: a set of board and pieces used for that game
examples: Dit ivoren-ebbenhouten damspel is een erfstuk

dreunen.e:
From Middle Dutch *dronen, from Old Dutch *drunen, from Frankish *drunnjan; the long vowel /øː/ precludes a derivation from a hypothetical Proto-Germanic *drunjaną.
Cognate to Middle Low German dronen, Old Norse drynja, and also related to Gothic 𐌳𐍂𐌿𐌽𐌾𐌿𐍃 (drunjus).

examples: De dader kon helaas ontkomen.

dader.e: 16th century, back-formation from misdader, goeddader, weldader. Equivalent to daad (“deed”) + -er. Compare German Täter (also a backformation).

s.def: a djembe, large hand drum played with both hands

djembé.e: Borrowed from French djembé, from Mandinka jembe.

examples: Jij maakt mij droef.

droef.e: From Middle Dutch droeve, droue, from Old Dutch *drōvi, *druovi, from Proto-West Germanic *drōbī, from Proto-Germanic *drōbuz (“turbid”). Compare English droff and German trüb.

examples: In de verte hoor ik het donderen.

s.def: to make a thunderous sound, especially with one's voice
examples: 'Hou daar nu mee op!', donderde de man.
De leerling verloor zijn evenwicht en donderde achterover van zijn stoel.

s.def: a village in Tynaarlo, Drenthe, Netherlands

donderen.e: From Middle Dutch donren, donderen. Equivalent to donder + -en.

examples: Pele scoorde het enige doelpunt van de wedstrijd.
We kwamen op achterstand door een eigen doelpunt.

s.def: any similarly complex and/or confusing construction, lay-out, situation etc.
examples: Wie vindt z'n weg nog in de doolhof van wetten?

doolhof.e: From dolen (“to wander, err”) + hof (“court, garden”).

s.def: requiem (Catholic mass in honor and remembrance of a deceased person)
examples: De familieleden woonden de dodenmis bij om hun overleden geliefde te herdenken.

s.def: aperture (something which restricts the diameter of the light path through one plane in an optical system)
diaphragm: the muscle between thorax and abdomen needed for breething

diafragma.e: From Middle Dutch diafragma, from Latin diaphragma, from Ancient Greek δῐᾰ́φρᾰγμᾰ (dĭắphrăgmă).

dashond.e: From das (“badger”) + hond (“hound, dog”), calque of German Dachshund (whence also English).

s.def: to dawdle, move slowly, not take action

dralen.e: From Middle Dutch drālen, draelen. Further etymology uncertain, but apparently related to East Frisian draueln, drölen (“to dawdle, loiter”), Westphalian drålen (“to dawdle, be slow moving”).

s.def: a debtor, party with a debit (due) on an account
examples: De gezamenlijke schulden van dubieuze debiteuren ondermijnen de kredietwaardigheid van hun crediteur

debiteur.e: Borrowed from Middle French debiteur, from Latin debitum, supine of debere (“to be due, have to, must”).

s.def: diabolic (showing wickedness typical of a devil)

diabolisch.e: Borrowed from French diabolique, from Latin diabolicus, from Koine Greek διαβολικός (diabolikós).

s.def: defeatism, acceptance of defeat or other misery without struggle

defaitisme.e: Borrowed from French défaitisme (cognate with English defeatism, German Defätismus etc.), calque of Russian пораженчество (poraženčestvo), coined in 1915. By surface analysis, defaite (“defeat”) + -isme (“-ism”).

second/third-person singular dependent-clause present indicative of deelnemen
singular dependent-clause past indicative of deelnemen
singular dependent-clause present subjunctive of deelnemen

examples: Het duizelt mij.

duizelen.e: From Middle Dutch duselen. By surface analysis, duizen + -elen, although duizen is not attested in Middle Dutch.

s.def: a rolling pin, rolling food preparation utensil fit to flatten dough
discretion (the quality of being discreet or circumspect)

discretie.e: From Middle Dutch discrecie, from Old French discrecion, from Late Latin discrētiō.

s.def: a planar support; a surface that supports something
an airfoil, the carrying plane of an airplane
support (within society or an organization), a base of support
examples: Voor die bezuinigingen is er te weinig draagvlak in de samenleving.

s.def: dating (process of estimating the age of something)

examples: De hulporganisatie biedt ondersteuning en behandeling voor drugsverslaafden die hun verslaving willen overwinnen.

dactyloscopie.e: Probably, probably either from English dactyloscopy or French dactyloscopie. Formed from Ancient Greek δάκτυλος (dáktulos, “finger”) + σκοπέω (skopéō, “I look at”).

dwergplaneet.e: From dwerg (“dwarf”) + planeet (“planet”), calque of English dwarf planet.

examples: Nou, wat zit je dwars?
Ik weet niet wat hem dwarszit.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Valkenswaard, North Brabant, Netherlands

dommelen.e: Unknown. May be the same as dommelen (“to buzz”), with influence from dom (“dumb”).

s.def: a river in North Brabant, Netherlands

dommel.e: Referenced in Latin by Willibrord as a river in Texandria (roughly, Brabant and its vicinity) by the name Duthmala (727 CE). Elsewhere it is attested as Dutmala around the same time. The second element is Old Dutch mala (“dale, lowland”) (found in placenames, compare Ezemaal, Watermaal, etc.); th...

examples: Het debuutalbum van deze vroege beatband is nauwelijks meer verkrijgbaar.

s.def: singular dependent-clause present subjunctive of doorgeven
any one scramble of a Rubik's cube

dryade.e: Borrowed from French dryade, from Latin dryas, from Ancient Greek δρυάς (druás).

s.def: a wrinkle that becomes visible when a person is deep in thought and furrows their forehead, brow in concentration

daltonisme.e: Borrowed from German Daltonismus or English Daltonism. Equivalent to Dalton + -isme, named after John Dalton.

examples: Er waren veel bloemen en kaarsen op de plek waar de dode werd gevonden.

s.def: a casualty, victim of a fatal killing, illness, accident etc.
examples: Na het ongeluk waren er meerdere doden te betreuren.

s.def: to break, break through, break in half
examples: Toen de dikke man op zijn stoel ging zitten, brak de stoel door.
De piloot heeft de geluidsbarriere doorbroken.

doorbreken.e: From Middle Dutch dorebreken. Equivalent to door + breken.

s.def: vagrant (animal occurring outside its species' usual range), usually a vagrant bird

dwaalgast.e: From dwalen (“to wander”) + gast (“guest”). Compare zomergast.

s.def: management (administration; the process or practice of managing)

directie.e: Borrowed from French direction, from Latin dīrēctiō.

diender.e: From Middle Dutch diender, variant of dienre. By surface analysis, dienen + er. The -d- is an instance of epenthesis commonly encountered between liquid consonants and vowels; compare for example doender, daalder.

deskundig.e: Univerbation of des (“of that”, genitive singular masculine/neuter of dat) + kundig (“knowledgeable, capable”).

s.def: inflection of duurder, the comparative degree of duur:
inflection of duurder, the comparative degree of duur:
inflection of duurder, the comparative degree of duur:

dreun.e: From dreunen (“to drone, boom, make a thud”).

s.def: sales (the totality of products or services sold in a given timeframe)

examples: Een welgemaakt en keurlyk draaiorgel.

draaiorgel.e: From draaien + orgel, calque of German Drehorgel. First attested in the 1780s.

diefstal.e: From late Middle Dutch diefstal, from Middle High German diepstale, from diep (“thief”) + stale (“theft”). The former part has the same origin as dief, the latter is from Old High German stāla, from Proto-Germanic *stēlō. Replaced the Middle Dutch word diefte, which still survives in some dialects.

s.def: a dormitory, especially in a monastery
an early Christian cemetery or necropolis
moisture or a particulate solid that passes through a barrier like a wall
a neighbourhood of Nieuwegein, Utrecht, Netherlands

doorslag.e: From Middle Dutch dōreslach. Equivalent to door + slag, after doorslaan.

devoot.e: From Middle Dutch devoot, from Old French devot, from Latin devotus.

s.def: to carry or transport further, to continue to transport
to import in order to reexport

doorvoeren.e: From door + voeren, calque of German durchführen.

droevig.e: From Middle Dutch droevich. Equivalent to droef + -ig.

definitief.e: From Middle Dutch definitijf, from Middle French definitif, from Old French definitif, from Latin dēfīnītīvus.

droefheid.e: From Middle Dutch droefheit. Equivalent to droef (“sad”) + -heid (“-ness”).

s.def: a dull impact; a slam, a pound, a blow

dof.e: Vowel shortening of doof (“deaf”), from Middle Dutch dôof, from Old Dutch *dōf, from Proto-West Germanic *daub, from Proto-Germanic *daubaz (“stunned, deaf”), from Proto-Indo-European *dʰewbʰ- (“to whisk, be obscured”). Compare Swedish dov (“matt, muted”), English dowf.

s.def: singular past indicative of doordenken (“to think through”)
past participle of doordenken (“to think through”)
singular dependent-clause past indicative of doordenken (“to keep thinking”)

daartussen.e: From Middle Dutch dâertusschen. Equivalent to daar + tussen.

stylish, distinguished, genteel (showing high social class)

deftig.e: Perhaps borrowed from Middle Low German [Term?]. Equivalent to deft + -ig. Ultimately from Proto-West Germanic *daftī (“fitting, suitable”).

examples: Dat verklaar ik desnoods onder ede.

desnoods.e: Univerbation of des + noods, genitive of nood.

s.def: Dacian (of, from or related to Dacia)
diploid (of a cell, having a pair of each type of chromosome)

examples: De politie heeft een beruchte drugsbaron gearresteerd tijdens een grote drugsoperatie.

donderbus.e: From Middle Dutch donrebusse. Equivalent to donder (“thunder”) + bus (“gun; box”).

examples: iemand op een dwaalspoor brengen: to mislead someone

s.def: Dronten (a village and municipality of Flevoland, Netherlands)

dronten.e: Attested as Dronten in 1950. Named after a former area near Kampen, which in turn probably derives from Middle Dutch dronten (“swell up, rise up”), here referring to alluvial land.

examples: Behalve deze afdelingen was er op de MVD nog een departement, dat zich specialiseerde in het verspreiden van valse geruchten en berichten. Dat was de afdeling „Desinformatie”.

desinformatie.e: First attested in the 1950s, becoming common in the late 1970s. Borrowed from Russian дезинформа́ция (dezinformácija), from French désinformation. Equivalent to des- + informatie.

s.def: a place where children or people who have disability are cared for; daycare, nursery

dagverblijf.e: Compound of dag (“day”) + verblijf (“a place to stay”).

Bij den dril (Cynocephalus leucophaeus) zijn de wijfjes en jongen veel bleeker gekleurd, met minder groen, dan de volwassen mannetjes.

s.def: drill (dense, stout fabric, often of linen or cotton)

dril.e: Likely borrowed from English drill or French drill (see the oldest quotation), perhaps from an African language.

deugdelijk.e: From Middle Dutch dogedelijc. Equivalent to deugd (“virtue”) + -e- + -lijk.

doseren.e: Borrowed from French doser, from Latin [Term?]. Equivalent to dosis + -eren.

dweperig.e: From dweper (“fanatic”) + -ig, or from dwepen (“to rave, fanatacize”) + -erig.

deemster.e: From Middle Dutch deemster, demster (“dark”), from Old Dutch *thimster (in the compound thimsternisse (“darkness”)), from Proto-West Germanic *þimstr.

s.def: a go-getter, one who persists or perseveres

domheid.e: From Middle Dutch dompheit. Equivalent to dom + -heid.

demonstratief.e: Borrowed from French démonstratif, from Latin dēmōnstrātīvus.

s.def: any being that made the universe out of primal matter, demiurge

demiurg.e: From New Latin dēmiūrgus (“demiurge”), from Ancient Greek δημιουργός (dēmiourgós, “craftsman”) after Platonic usage.

doorzien.e: From Middle Dutch doresien. Equivalent to door- + zien.

diepvries.e: From diep + vries, partial calque of English deep freeze. Vries is not a noun in Dutch, but it was rather taken from the stem of the verb vriezen.

diepvriezen.e: From diepvries + -en, though as if diep + vriezen. Calque of English deep-freeze.

doorsnee.e: Contraction of doorsnede: equivalent to door + snee.

devies.e: From Middle Dutch devise, from Middle French devise, from Old French devise. The financial senses were borrowed from German Devise, from French devise.

dijken.e: First attested as In de Dijcken in 1505. Borrowed from West Frisian Diken, derived from the plural form of dyk (“levee”).

diabeticus.e: From diabetes + -icus or borrowed from medical Latin.

d'r.e: Reduced form of dier (“her, their, the latter's”).

dukaat.e: From Middle Dutch ducaet, from older ducas, possibly via Middle French ducat, from Italian ducato.

s.def: prepared text intended to be read aloud at a meeting or presentation (e.g. in class)

degenkrab.e: Compound of degen (“épée, duelling sword”) + krab (“crab”).

domproost.e: From Middle Dutch doemproost. Equivalent to dom + proost.

diëtair.e: Probably borrowed from French diétaire. By surface analysis, dieet + -air.

dankbaarlijk.e: From Middle Dutch dancbaerlike. Equivalent to dankbaar + -lijk.

dyon.e: Modified from Dijon. The digraph is fairly rare among European languages, but it is common in Dutch, and in older Dutch spellings it is generally interchangeable with . Thus, some people may think is "too Dutch" for a foreign word, and that it therefore must be a misspelling for . Compare byou.

dading.e: Inherited from Middle Dutch dāghedinc, dādinc, dāghedinghe or dādinghe. Re-analysed in Dutch dag (“day”) + geding (“lawsuit”).

dat kan bruin niet trekken.e: Literally, “[the] brown [horse] can't pull that”. From older dat kan grauw niet trekken, which referred to a donkey (or a grey horse according to some authors). Several authors include daar moet een bruin voor wezen (“a brown [horse] has to be in front of that”) behind the variant about the donke...
f: dat kan de bruine niet trekken (alternative)
dat kon bruin niet trekken (alternative, past)
dat kon de bruine niet trekken (alternative, past)

doodtij.e: From dood + tij. Compare French marée de morte eau.

examples:
1726 April, review of "Hekeldichten en brieven van Q. Horatius Flaccus", "Maendelyke uittreksels, of Boekzaal der geleerde werelt", vol. 22, publ. by Gerard onder de Linden, page 453.
Zoo dat het den Heere Huidecoper in dien opzigte aen geene ſtof zou hebben ontbroken: maer wat zwerm van verachtelyke ſteekdichten, die vry ſtomp zouden zyn geweeſt, zou hy zich daer door op den hals gehaelt hebben, wat ligue wat ſamenzweering zouden de Drekpoëetjes niet tegen hem gemaekt hebben, zoo wel als zy tegen Despreaux gedaen hebben, om hunne prulſchriften te beſchermen.

durfkapitaal.e: Compound of durven or durf + kapitaal.

doeltrap.e: Compound of doel (“goal”) + trap (“kick”), probably a calque of English goal kick.

dolkstootlegende.e: From dolkstoot + legende, calque of German Dolchstoßlegende.

durfkapitalisme.e: Compound of durven or durf + kapitalisme.

durfkapitalist.e: Compound of durven or durf + kapitalist.

deactiveren.e: Probably borrowed. Equivalent to de- + activeren. Compare German deaktivieren, French désactiver.

donderstorm.e: From donder + storm, calque of English thunderstorm.

dovenetel.e: Univerbation of dove (“deaf”) + netel (“nettle”). As with English deadnettle, the name refers to the fact that these plants (particularly Lamium album) resemble nettles, but lack any sting. Compare German Taubnessel.

doeidoei.e: Reduplication of doei; compare hoihoi and English bye-bye.

deemoed.e: Borrowed from German Demut or Low German [Term?].

de pijp uit gaan.e: Literally, “to go out (of) the pipe”, in reference to a rabbit hole.

one who killed someone by driving (e.g. running over or colliding)

despoot.e: Borrowed from French despote, from Middle French [Term?], from Latin despota, from Ancient Greek δεσπότης (despótēs).

examples: De marine gebruikte een dieptebom om de onderzeeër aan te vallen.
Ze vonden een oude dieptebom op de zeebodem.

to drop dead, to die suddenly

examples: Val dood!

doodvallen.e:
From dood + vallen.
Compare English drop dead.

denktank.e: From denken + tank, calque of English think tank.

dereguleren.e: Borrowed from English deregulate. Equivalent to de- + reguleren.

deputeren.e: From Middle Dutch deputeren, from Old French deputer, from Latin deputō.

donderpreek.e: Compound of donder (“thunder”) or donderen (“to thunder; to shout”) + preek (“sermon”).

dijzig.e: Uncertain. Only found in Modern Dutch. Compare Saterland Frisian díezig (“slightly foggy, hazy”), German Low German diesig (“foggy, hazy”), German diesig (“hazy, misty”), Danish disig (“hazy”), Swedish disig (“hazy”).

doorkoelen.e: From door + koelen or door- + koelen.

devotie.e: From Middle Dutch devotie, from Old French devotion, from Latin dēvōtiō.

s.def: genitive singular masculine/neuter of deze; of this

doolweg.e: From Middle Dutch doolwech. Equivalent to dolen + weg.

dienstknecht.e: From Middle Dutch dienstcnecht. Equivalent to dienst + knecht.

diagonaal.e: Borrowed from Middle French diagonal (from Latin) or Latin diagonālis, from Ancient Greek διαγώνιος (diagṓnios, “from angle to angle”), from διά (diá, “across”) + γωνία (gōnía, “angle”).

dijenkletser.e: Compound of dij (“thigh”) + -en- + kletsen (“to smack, to slap”) + -er.

Zij kunnen tegen het advies ingaan, zij het onder voorwaarden: blijkt de leerling te hoog te hebben gegrepen, dan wordt het dringende advies omgezet in een dwingend advies. In dat geval zal de leerling alsnog het teamadvies moeten volgen.

dwingend advies.e:
From dwingend (“forced”) + advies (“advice”), either a pun on or a misconstruction of dringend advies (“urgent advice”).
This is a contradictio in terminis.

doornik.e: (Lingewaard) First attested as apud tornacum towards the end of the 11th century. Etymology uncertain. Potentially a relic from a pre-Germanic substrate language. It is unlikely that the toponym is derived from Doornik in Belgium or from a Celtic word with an -acum suffix. Compare Indoornik.

s.def: a neighbourhood of Almere, Flevoland, Netherlands

danswijk.e: * (neighbourhood) Compound of dans (“dance”) and wijk (“neighbourhood, quarter”). The neighbourhood's streets are named after various ballet dancers.

doodsbed.e: From Middle Dutch dôotbedde. Equivalent to dood + -s- + bed.

drup.e: This form is very likely based on druppel, which is a suffixed derivate of drop that displays the effects of i-umlaut due to the suffix -el, which triggers it; drup is otherwise not at all an expected reflex of Proto-Germanic *drupô, as the short Germanic *u regularly yields Dutch o, not u.

druppen.e: From Middle Dutch droppen, from Old Dutch droppon, from Proto-West Germanic *droppōn, from Proto-Germanic *druppōną.

doornenkroon.e: From Middle Dutch dornecrône. Equivalent to doorn + -en- + kroon.

doornroosje.e: From doorn (“thorn”) + roosje (“little rose”), calque of German Dornröschen. The anomalous stress follows the German.

duikbril.e: Compound of duiken (“to dive”) + bril (“spectacles, goggles”).

examples: En de HEERE zeide tot Mozes: Verzamel Mij zeventig mannen uit de oudsten van Israel, dewelke gij weet, dat zij de oudsten des volks en deszelfs ambtlieden zijn; en gij zult hen brengen voor de tent der samenkomst, en zij zullen zich daar bij u stellen.

dewelke.e: From Middle Dutch dewelke, later variant of diewelke. Equivalent to a univerbation of de + welke.

dopingzondaar.e: Compound of doping (“doping”) + zondaar (literally “sinner”). The latter is used here in the sense of “offender” as in verkeerszondaar (“traffic offender”). Compare German Dopingsünder.

derwisj.e: Borrowed from German Derwisch, from Ottoman Turkish درویش, from Persian درویش.

delfzijl.e:
First attested as in delfzilen in 1303. Compound of the hydronym Delf (derived from delven (“dig, excavate”)) and zijl (“discharge sluice”).
See also Dutch Low Saxon Delfziel.

s.def: a city and former municipality of Noardeast-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

dokkum.e: First attested as tochingen in 802-822. Potentially derived from the dative plural form of Old Frisian Dokko (a personal name) suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ingi- or, alternatively, from dokke (“type of plant, dock”). Compare Docking.

s.def: The name of a locality in the vicinity of Ukkel

dievoet.e:
The surname is derived from the place name in Belgium. As -voet in placenames on occasion alternates with -voort or similar, there are two suggested origins:
* Celtic, meaning "divine ford," akin to Proto-Celtic *deiwos + *ɸritus. This would make the name similar to the French place name Jort fro...

part of the surname (Netherlands) Van Dievoort, (Belgium) Vandievoort

de cocksdorp.e: Attested as COCKSDORP (DE) in 1841. From de Cock (a surname) + -s- + dorp (“village, settlement”). Named after Nicolaas Josephus de Cock (1804-1848), a shipowner who founded a company to develop the land around the settlement in 1835.

s.def: a village in Texel, North Holland, Netherlands

den burg.e: Potentially attested as osterburghem and ostarburghem in 918-948, attested with certainty as brochkerspel [read: borchkerspel] in 1377. Derived from Old Dutch burg (“fortified settlement, castle”).

a village and former municipality of Westerveld, Drenthe, Netherlands

diever.e:
First attested as deuere in 1181. The toponym may be related to Middle Dutch *beduven (“dip in water, cover in water”). Alternatively, the toponym may derive from an ancient hydronym.
See also Dutch Low Saxon Dever.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Westerveld, Drenthe, Netherlands

dwingeloo.e:
First attested as twingelo in 1181. Compound of an unclear first element and lo (“light forest”). The first element has been interpreted as Proto-Germanic *thwangi (“belt”) and Middle Dutch dwing (“legal district, reserved area”). Compare Balloo.
See also Dutch Low Saxon Dwingel.

s.def: a village in Ouder-Amstel, North Holland, Netherlands

duivendrecht.e:
First attested as doevendrecht in 1308. From Middle Dutch Dovendrecht. Compound of Middle Dutch Doeve (a personal name) and -drecht (“ferry, watercourse”). The first element was gradually reinterpreted to the plural form of duif (“pigeon”).
Compare Duiveland, Duiven, Duivendijke and Duivenvoorde.

dinnetje.e: Clipping of vriendinnetje, diminutive of vriendin.

s.def: a village and former municipality of Utrechtse Heuvelrug, Utrecht, Netherlands

driebergen-rijsenburg.e: Compound of Driebergen + Rijsenburg. Driebergen is first attested as thriberge in 1159. Rijsenburg is first attested as risenborch and risenborgh in 1297. Driebergen is derived from a compound of Middle Dutch drie (“three”) and the plural form of berch (“hill, elevated place”); the former village...

s.def: a village and municipality of Utrecht, Netherlands

de bilt.e: First attested as die bilt in 1307. Potentially derived from an ablauted variant of Middle Dutch belt (“slightly elevated place”). Not derived from bult (“slightly elevated place, bump”) or from Latin balteus (“belt”).

demonisch.e: Probably borrowed from German dämonisch. Equivalent to demoon + -isch.

demoon.e: Borrowed from French démon (“demon”), from Middle French [Term?], from Latin daemōn (“demon; (protective) spirit”), from Ancient Greek δαίμων (daímōn, “demon; (protective) spirit”).

drogredenaar.e: Compound of drog + redenaar, after drogreden (“fallacy”).

doceet.e: Probably borrowed from Latin Docetae, ultimately from Ancient Greek δοκέω (dokéō, “to appear, to seem”).

The name of the neighbourhood is first attested as Doornbosch in 1899. The toponym derives from doornbos (“thorn bush”). Originally the name of a farm.
The surname derives from doornbos (“thorn bush”) or from one of several toponyms.

darwiniaan.e: Probably borrowed from English. Equivalent to Darwin + -iaan (“-ian”).

decreteren.e: From Middle Dutch decreteren, from Middle French décréter.

deductie.e: From Middle Dutch deductie, from Latin dēductiō.

delicaat.e: From Middle Dutch delicaet, from Middle French delicat, from Latin dēlicātus (“alluring”).

demonteren.e: Borrowed from French démonter. Equivalent to de- + monteren.

den helder.e: First attested as de Helderbuyert in 1577. Etymology unknown. Unlikely to be derived from hil (“hill, elevated place”), helle (“low-lying land”) or helde (“slope”). Potentially derived from the surname Helder.

deserteur.e: Borrowed from French déserteur, from Middle French déserteur, from Old French [Term?].

doctoreren.e: From Latin doctorare (“to award or attain a doctorate”).

Compare Duiven, Duivendijke, Duivendrecht and...

First attested as drieskire and dreeskiere in 1206. Etymology unknown. The toponym appears to be a compound of an unknown first element and a term cognate with Old English scira (“shire, legal district”).

domburg.e: First attested as dumburch in 1181-1210. Compound of Proto-Germanic *dun- (“dune”) and burg (“fortified settlement, castle”).

de groe.e:
First attested as St Maarten ter Groede in the 16th century. Derived from Middle Dutch groede (“overgrown alluvial land deposited by the sea”). Compare Graauw, Groede and Groet.

de schenge.e: Attested as De Schengen in 1838-1857. Derived from an ancient hydronym, which may in turn derive from Proto-Indo-European *(s)keng- (“bent, sideways”).

dijkwel.e: First attested as dicwale in 1264. Compound of dijk (“levee”) and Middle Dutch wael (“kolk lake”).

duivenhoek.e: Attested as Duivels hoec around 1660. Compound of duivel (“devil”) and hoek (“corner”). The first element was gradually reinterpreted to duif (“dove”) in an effort to lessen the negative connotations of the toponym. Compare Roverberg.

dullaert.e: Attested as Dullaert in 1542-1546. Derived from Middle Dutch dullaert (“madman, savage”). Originally a hydronym; the watercourse was named for its unpredictable character.

de knol.e: Attested as De Knol in 1838-1857. Derived from knol (“extremity of a levee”).

s.def: a hamlet in Terneuzen, Zeeland, Netherlands

de ratte.e: Attested as Ratte (De) in 1899. Apparently named after a kolk lake called De Ratteput ("the Rats' well"). See also De Muis.

de sterre.e: Attested as Het Sterretje in 1838-1857. Probably named after a former café.

drieschouwen.e: Attested as De 3 Schouwen in 1838-1857. Compound of drie (“three”) and the plural form of schouw (“chimney”). Apparently named after three former salterns.

draaibrug.e:

de munte.e: Attested as Munte in 1936. Derived from Old Dutch munte (“hill serving as a place of refuge during flooding”). Originally the name of a field.

delfstrahuizen.e: First attested as nye delfsterhuisen and olde delfsterhuisen in 1408. Borrowed from a compound of West Frisian delf (“dug watercourse”) and the plural form of hûs (“house”). The Dutch form of the toponym was borrowed from an earlier version of the Frisian name.

de rijlst.e: First attested as De Kijl[read: De Rijl] in 1700. Borrowed from West Frisian De Rylst, derived in turn from ryl (“clear strip of land in a heathland”).

delburen.e: First attested as Delburen in 1861. Calque of West Frisian Delbuorren, a compound of del (“dale”) and the plural form of buer (“house, cottage”).

deersum.e: First attested as deersum in 1427. Borrowed from West Frisian Dearsum, likely derived from a compound of Old Frisian *Diuara (a personal name) and hēm (“home, settlement”).

dedgum.e: First attested as deddingiuuerbe in 855. The second element was gradually replaced by hēm (“home, settlement”). The Dutch form ...

de bieren.e: First attested as De Bieren in 1543. Borrowed from West Frisian De Bieren, potentially derived from the plural form of Old Frisian buer (“house, cottage”).

s.def: a hamlet in Súdwest-Fryslân, Friesland, Netherlands

de grits.e: Borrowed from West Frisian De Grits, potentially derived from Old Frisian Gert (a personal name) suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ingi-.

de klieuw.e: First attested as Klijw and Cliw in 1511. Borrowed from West Frisian De Kliuw, derived in turn from klief (“type of lock”).

de band.e: First attested as in der Band in 1511. Borrowed from West Frisian De Band, derived in turn from Old Frisian bant (“area, inhabited area”).

de blokken.e: First attested as dae blocken in 1493. Calque of West Frisian De Blokken, derived in turn from the plural form of Old Frisian blok (“farmland enclosed by a ditch or fence”). Compare Blokum.

de hel.e: First attested in 1700. Borrowed from West Frisian De Hel, of uncertain etymology. Potentially derived from Old Frisian hil (“hill, elevated place”), hel (“low-lying swampland”) or hellen (“place where turf is dried”).

de kat.e: Borrowed from West Frisian Kat, derived in turn from kat (“type of sluice”). Named after a nearby sluice.

* (Noord-Holland) Attested as Nes in 1839-1859. Derived from nes (“headland, spit”).

de rode schuur.e: Calque of West Frisian Rea Skuorre, derived from rea (“red”) and skuorre (“barn”). Originally the name of a farmhouse.

de weeren.e: Potentially attested as werem in 1379, attested with certainty as weerem in 1431. Borrowed from West Frisian De Wearen, derived in turn from the dative plural form of Old Frisian weer (“artificial mound used for habitation”).

dijksterburen.e: Potentially attested as Dixherne in 1543, attested with certainty as Dycksterhuysen in 1664. Calque of West Frisian Dyksterbuorren, a compound of dyk (“levee”) and the plural form of buer (“house, cottage”).

doniaburen.e: First attested as Doinbuyren in the 17th century. Calque of West Frisian Doanjebuorren, derived from a compound of Donia (a surname) and the plural form of buer (“house, cottage”).

doniawier.e: First attested as Doeygwier and Donya waer in 1543. Calque of West Frisian Doanjewier, derived from a compound of Old Frisian Donia (a surname) and wier (“artificial mound used for habitation”).

draaisterhuizen.e: First attested as toe der draija in 1486. Calque of West Frisian Draaisterhuzen, a compound of the toponym Ter Draai or the hydronym t Dray and the plural form of hûs (“house”).

deinum.e: First attested as deynim in 1397. Borrowed from West Frisian Deinum, potentially derived in turn from a compound of Old Frisian Dago (a personal name) suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ingi- and hēm (“home, settlement”).

dronrijp.e: First attested with certainty as dronryp around 1243. Borrowed from West Frisian Dronryp, potentially derived in turn from a compound of Old Frisian *Drenno (a personal name) suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ingi- and ripe (“edge, bank”).

dongjum.e: First attested as thuninghum in the 13th century. Derived from a compound of Old Frisian Dunna (a personal name) suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ingi- and hēm (“home, settlement”). The Dutch form of the toponym was borrowed from an earlier version of the Frisian name.

dijkshoek.e: Potentially attested as dijkshorn in 1287. Calque of West Frisian Dykshoeke, a compound of dyk (“levee”) and hoeke (“corner”).

dijksterhuizen.e: First attested as Dyckstrahusen in 1502. Calque of West Frisian Dyksterhuzen, a compound of dyk (“levee”) and the plural form of hûs (“house”).

s.def: a hamlet in Waadhoeke, Friesland, Netherlands

doijum.e: First attested as dodoghema buren in 1402. Derived from a compound of Old Frisian Dodo (a personal name) suffixed with the collectivising suffix -ingi- and hēm (“home, settlement”). The Dutch form of the toponym was borrowed from an earlier version of the Frisian name.

de blijnse.e: First attested as bloedige blincxe in 1546. Borrowed from West Frisian De Blynse, derived from a hydronym cognate to blinken (“shine, sparkle”).

djiera.e: From Caribbean Hindustani jirá, ultimately from Sanskrit जीरक (jīraka).
