dubbel liggen.e: Literally, “to lie doubled up”, from being overcome with laughter.

s.def: to rebuke, scold, reprimand severely, put someone in their place
examples: Ze waste de spijbelaar de oren.
De dictator snoerde critici de mond.

s.def: synonym of griel (“Eurasian stone-curlew, Burhinus oedicnemus”)
to hold one's own, be assertive
examples: Maak je geen zorgen over haar, zij laat zich de kaas echt niet van het brood eten.

de kaas niet van het brood laten eten.e: Literally, “not to let [anyone else] eat the cheese off one’s bread”. Compare German sich die Butter vom Brot nehmen lassen.

s.def: an inhabitant of the Dutch province of Drenthe
an artificial wetland archipelago in lake IJsselmeer, located in the province Noord-Holland, The Netherlands, not far from Andijk
to reign supreme, be the best or finest
examples: Deze saison spant de kroon onder de bieren van deze brouwerij.
Ik heb veel slechte films gezien, maar deze flutzooi spant de kroon.

de kroon spannen.e: From Middle Dutch crone spannen. Kroon here refers to a victor's laurels.

s.def: a resident in an EU country other than Belgium who does not hold citizenship of an EU country
a refugee who has fled a country where he or she has resided but does not hold citizenship

dag in dag uit.e: Compare English day in, day out, German tagein tagaus, Norwegian dag inn og dag ut.

s.def: that is to say, that is, i.e.

dat wil zeggen.e: Literally, “that wants to say”. Compare Danish det vil sige etc.

de stuipen op het lijf jagen.e: Literally, “to bring the shivers onto [someone's] body”.

s.def: to hit the nail on the head

de spijker op de kop slaan.e: Literally, “to hit the nail on the head”, as in English hit the nail on the head.

examples: In noodgevallen kunt u banden van het fixatiemateriaal doorsnijden met een reddingsmes.
Je moet niet stoppen met knippen! Je moet doorknippen totdat je het hele figuurtje hebt uitgeknipt.

s.def: to be disadvantaged or victimized; to have a problem; to be screwed
examples: Als je aangeschoten achter het stuur gepakt wordt, ben je pas echt de sigaar.

s.def: to have a problem; to be fucked
examples: Als ze die coke in je portemonnee vinden, ben je de lul.

dravik.e:
From Middle Dutch dravik; further origin uncertain:
* Possibly a derivation from the same source as draf (“dregs from grain”), and closely related to dreps (“Bromus secalinus”). For the suffix, compare herik, wederik, and so forth; -ik is a common element in plant names.
* A connection with Latin...

dreps.e: Closely related to dravik, but with a different suffix.

s.def: to celebrate the halfway point of the working week on Wednesday, for example by having lunch or going for drinks after work
examples: Het is alweer woensdag, kom je mee uit lunchen om de week doormidden te zagen?

de week doormidden zagen.e: Literally, “to saw the week through the middle”.

droeftoeter.e: Compound of droef (“sad”) + toeter (“horn”). Compare roeptoeter.

s.def: to kill the goose that lays the golden eggs

de kip met de gouden eieren slachten.e: Literally, “to butcher the chicken with the golden eggs”. From a fable by Aesop; see English kill the goose that lays the golden eggs.

s.def: to salvage what can be salvaged, to save as much as possible, to do damage control, to cut one's losses, to minimize one's losses

de meubels redden.e: Calque of French sauver les meubles.

s.def: any, ever (as in anywhere, wherever and suchlike)
examples: hoe dan ook
wie dan ook

s.def: Used other than figuratively or idiomatically: see dan, ook.
examples: Hij ging weg en ik ben dan ook weggegaan.

s.def: to miss the boat, to miss the bus

demoraliseren.e: Borrowed from French démoraliser. Equivalent to de- + moraliseren.

diversiteitsgeneuzel.e: From diversiteit (“diversity”) + -s- + geneuzel (“nonsense, drivel, babble, twaddle”); an expression of diversity fatigue.

s.def: to cross swords (to quarrel or argue; to have a dispute)
to gain popularity, to enjoy increasing success

de wind in de zeilen hebben.e: Compare French avoir le vent en poupe.

s.def: all participants in a competition, competitors taken collectively

dunheid.e: From Middle Dutch dunheit. Compare German Dünnheit. By surface analysis, dun + -heid.

s.def: to charge a cost for something, to pass a cost on someone, to bill
worth the trouble, worth it, worth one's while
examples: Het is de moeite waard.

de moeite waard.e: Literally, “worth the effort”. Compare French valoir la peine.

s.def: to move on, to progress to a higher level
examples: Met het contactpersoon kunt u bespreken of doorstromen naar een hoger schoolniveau tot de mogelijkheden behoort.
De nieuwe weg moet ervoor zorgen dat het verkeer weer vlot kan doorstromen.
Het laboratorium is allang verlaten, maar dat water stroomt nog gewoon door, in de kanalen, buizen en stuwen.
De Kromme Rijn doorstroomt de stad Utrecht en gaat over in de Vecht.

s.def: to double the quantity (especially a bet)

doubleren.e: Borrowed from Middle French doubler with the suffix -eren.

s.def: to slip through the cracks, to fall through the cracks, to slip through the net

door de mazen van het net glippen.e: Literally, “to slip through the meshes of the net”. Compare French passer à travers les mailles du filet.

dientengevolge.e: Univerbation of dien + ten gevolge, containing a fossilized dative form of gevolg. Compare German infolgedessen.

s.def: a left-wing or progressive man, particularly one perceived to be a virtue signaller
examples: Coordinate term: deuggleuf

deugkneus.e: From deugen (“to be moral”) + kneus (“twerp”). The verb deugen has connotations of sanctimoniousness in contemporary Dutch right-wing populist discourse.

s.def: a left-wing or progressive woman, particularly one perceived to be a virtue signaller
examples: Coordinate term: deugkneus

deuggleuf.e: From deugen (“to be moral”) + gleuf (“slit, vagina”). The verb deugen has connotations of sanctimoniousness in contemporary Dutch right-wing populist discourse.

s.def: a left-wing or progressive person, particularly one perceived to be a virtue signaller

deugmens.e: From deugen (“to be moral”) + mens (“person, human”). The verb deugen has connotations of sanctimoniousness in contemporary Dutch right-wing populist discourse.

s.def: to scold or berate heavily; to swear at someone intensely
all the, used with a comparative as an intensifier
examples: des te meer

s.def: the ... the, forms a parallel comparative
examples: Des te meer, des te beter!
Hoe eerder, des te beter!
Anatoly Karpov heeft een ietwat katachtige kop. Dat viel destemeer op, toen hij werd omringd door leden van de cast van de musical Cats. Hij viel toen namelijk helemaal niet meer op.
De minder prettige omstandigheid had echter dit voordeel, dat de receptie in de veel te kleine, maar goed verwarmde ruimten van de „Sneek" destebeter werd gewaardeerd.

doodsaai.e: Compound of dood + saai, with dood here acting as an intensifier (compare doodmoe).

destructie.e: From Middle Dutch destructie, from Old French destrucion, from Latin dēstructiōnem (accusative singular of dēstructiō).

s.def: to destroy (euthanize, put down; mainly used for farm animals)
a thousand and one (a great many)

examples: De kous is af, het is tijd om naar huis te gaan.
Wat mij betreft is de kous daarmee af.

dekoloniaal.e: Borrowed from English decolonial. By surface analysis, de- + koloniaal.

s.def: to kill, put an end to
examples: We hebben dat project de nek omgedraaid.

de nek omdraaien.e: Literally, “to wring the neck”, referring to snapping the neck of poultry to kill the animal quickly.

s.def: Said when there is little food left or, by extension, of the living conditions of people that are living in poverty.
examples: Ik ben vergeten boodschappen te halen; nu liggen de muizen dood voor de kast.
De muizen liggen dood voor de kast bij dat gezin sinds de zorgkosten de pan uit zijn gerezen.

de muizen liggen dood voor de kast.e: Literally, “the mice are lying dead by the cupboard”.

differentiaal.e: Ultimately related to Latin differentia and differentiālis. Compare English differential.

examples: De digitale maatschapij vraagt om professionals die willen doorleren.
Het is avond, je hebt de hele dag gestudeerd. Nu komt het belangrijkste: slaap. Niet de hele nacht doorleren, slapen!

detectie.e: Borrowed from English detection or French détection. Equivalent to detecteren + -tie.

dedicatie.e: From Latin dēdicātiō. Equivalent to dediceren + -atie.

s.def: Jacobaea vulgaris subsp. dunensis, a subspecies of common ragwort

duinkruiskruid.e: From duin (“dune”) + kruiskruid (“plant currently or formerly of genus Senecio”). So named because this subspecies is common near the coast.

s.def: synonym of de sigaar zijn (“to have a problem; to be disadvantaged; to be screwed”)

de pineut zijn.e: Uncertain. The provenance of pineut is unclear; it may derive from English peanut.

s.def: an ultimate sacrifice (an offer for a comprise in which the petitioner concedes far-reaching concessions)
A problem is rarely as grave as it seems at first sight.

de soep wordt nooit zo heet gegeten als.e: Literally, “the soup is never eaten as hot as it is served”.
f: de soep wordt nooit zo heet gegeten als zij wordt opgediend (alternative)
de soep wordt nooit zo heet gegeten als hij wordt opgediend (alternative)
de soep wordt niet zo heet gegeten als ze wordt opgediend (alternative)
de soep wordt niet zo heet gegeten als zij wordt opgediend (alternative)
de soep wordt niet zo heet gegeten als hij wordt opgediend (alternative)

s.def: to sprint away from the other competitors in a race
a sudden sprint away from the other competitors

demarrage.e: Borrowed from French démarrage. Equivalent to demarreren + -age.

s.def: declaration (written or oral indication of a fact, opinion, intention, belief, etc.)
declaration (list of items for various legal purposes)

declaratie.e: From Middle Dutch declaracie, borrowed from French déclaration. Equivalent to declareren + -atie.

declaratief.e: Borrowed from French déclaratif. Equivalent to declaratie + -ief.

s.def: to soak fully, drench through and through
examples: De regenval was zwaar genoeg om zelfs mijn jas te doorweken.
Door de oefening was ik helemaal doorweekt van het zweet.

duizendklapper.e: Compound of duizend + klappen + -er. The name is an exaggeration.

examples: Pubers denken altijd dat ze de wijsheid in pacht hebben.

de wijsheid in pacht hebben.e: Literally, “to have the wisdom in lease”. Compare German die Weisheit gepachtet haben.

de lakens uitdelen.e: Literally, “to distribute the sheets of cloth”.

s.def: of perishable products available at an auction, to be destroyed or used for other purposes when left unsold
to freak out, lose one's mind, go crazy

s.def: double-sided (using or able to be used on both sides)

driewerf.e: From Middle Dutch driewerf, driewerven, from Old Dutch thriewerf. A univerbation of drie (“three”) + werf (“time[s], occasion[s]”).

examples: Coordinate term: mensenleven
Velen vinden dat onnodig verlies van dierenlevens moet worden voorkomen.

deflatie.e: Borrowed from German Deflation in the late 19th century in the geological sense. The economic sense is from the 1920s and is borrowed from English deflation or French déflation.

s.def: dirty chai (chai latte with shot of espresso)

dirty chai.e: Unadapted borrowing from English dirty chai.

s.def: Totally normal; not even remotely remarkable.

examples: Ik snap dat veiligheid belangrijk is, maar je kan ook doorslaan.

s.def: diadem (ornamental headband worn as a badge of royalty)

diadeem.e: From Middle Dutch diademe, from Old French diadème.

examples: O-ve-ral schoenen. Wie links kijkt, ziet de meest exclusieve Nikes en wie naar rechts kijkt, ziet Jordans staan met een prijskaartje erop met drie nullen.

drie nullen.e: Literally three zeroes, as a number with three zeroes must be at least 1000.

thick, thick as thieves, tight-knit, palsy, buddy-buddy
examples: Die zien elkaar elke dag, dat is dikke mik.
De broers waren altijd al dikke mik met elkaar.

dikke mik.e: Not entirely certain, but potentially literally “thick sandwich”, i.e. a richly-filled sandwich, similar to the formation of other food-related expressions that convey amicability such as koek en ei, or the Italian expression essere come pane e cacio.

s.def: to dwindle, to decline in number or size

dwijnen.e: From Middle Dutch dwinen, from Old Dutch *dwīnan, from Proto-West Germanic *dwīnan, from Proto-Germanic *dwīnaną.

to gush, be overly infatuated or enamored

dwepen.e: Likely denominative from obsolete dweep (“fool”) + -en, from Middle Dutch dweep, dwaep, of uncertain origin but possibly related to dwaas (“fool”).

s.def: to laugh one's head off, die laughing
examples: Ik heb me doodgelachen toen ik die aflevering keek.

doodlachen.e: Compound of dood (“dead”) + lachen (“to laugh”). Compare West Frisian dealaitsje, German totlachen.

deskundoloog.e: From deskundige (“expert”) + -oloog (“-ologist”), a somewhat irregular affixation (expected would be *deskundigoloog).

a-three; alternative form of drie (“three”), used when counting rhythmically

dwerghaver.e: Compound of dwerg (“dwarf”) + haver (“oat”). The word haver is used in botanical compounds not only for Avena spp., but also some other grasses.

s.def: to recoil, flinch, pull back, move backwards

deinzen.e: From Middle Dutch deinsen. The -n- is often cited as being secondary, as deisen is also common with the same meaning in Middle Dutch, but this is uncertain. Further etymology is unclear.

examples: Het plan is dikke prima, niks aan veranderen.

door to door (from one household or business to the next)

dossiervreter.e: Compound of dossier (“dossier”) + vreten (“to hog, gobble, wolf down”) + -er.

droedelen.e: From droedel (“droodle”) + -en; alternatively a direct borrowing from the verb usage of English droodle.

examples: Meerdere opties passeerden de revue, maar uiteindelijk kwamen we er niet uit.
Hij liet allerlei mogelijkheden de revue passeren.

duitse landvast.e: Compound of Duits (“German”) + landvast (“hawser”). The use of this term is based on the humouristic remark that German sailors would often connect their boats to a power source even before properly connecting it to the shore with a hawser.

diuretisch.e: From Latin diūrēticus, from Ancient Greek διουρητικός (diourētikós).

diureticum.e: From Latin diūrēticum, neuter singular of diūrēticus.

desgelijks.e: From Middle Dutch desgelikes. By surface analysis, des + gelijk + -s.

s.def: illustrious, august (honorific, mostly used of nobility)

doorluchtig.e: From Middle Dutch dorelichtich, a calque of Latin perillūstris.

dragon's blood (red resin obtained from various plants)
A dragon tree, particularly Dracaena draco and Dracaena cinnabari.

dorpel.e: From Middle Dutch dorpel. Further origin uncertain.

examples: Je moet nog driehonderd meter doorfietsen om bij de juiste afslag te komen.

examples: Als je geen lid bent, moet je de volle mep betalen.

dag des heren.e: Literally, “day of the Lord”, a calque of Latin diēs Dominicus.

examples: Het is me niet gelukt het plantje te determineren.

descriptie.e: Borrowed from French description, from Latin dēscrīptiō.

descriptief.e: Borrowed from French descriptif. By surface analysis, descriptie + -ief.

decernaat.e: Calque of German Dezernat (“department”), equivalent to decerneren + -aat.

examples: Na het ongeluk was het kind helemaal de kluts kwijt.

s.def: a belt that carries something or that allows you to carry something

dubbelchecken.e: Partial calque of English double check. By surface analysis, dubbel + checken.

ditjes en datjes.e: Literally, “little thises and little thats”; ditje and datje are diminutives of the pronouns/determiners dit and dat and are not used outside of this fixed expression.

speaking (indication that the person requested on the telephone call is the same as the one who is currently speaking)
my ass!, yeah, right!, I don't think so!
examples: Ja dikke lul, dat gaat dus echt niet gebeuren.

declameren.e: Borrowed from French déclamer, from Latin dēclāmāre.

declamatie.e: Borrowed from French déclamation, from Latin dēclāmātiō.
